title: Pull-Out Drawers for an Existing Cabinet
summary: A technique for retrofitting a fixed cabinet shelf into a side-slide pull-out drawer, sized to your own cabinet opening rather than a fixed spec.
description:
This isn't a plan with a fixed cut list — it's a technique for converting an existing cabinet opening into a pull-out drawer, and every dimension in it comes from measuring your own cabinet rather than a printed number. The one non-negotiable requirement is a genuinely square opening: side-mounted slides are unforgiving of a racked or out-of-square cabinet, and a drawer that isn't perfectly square will jam going in and out no matter how good the slides are.

Build the drawer box about 1" smaller than the opening in both height and width to give the slides clearance, using 1/2" plywood ripped to width for the shelves, front, and back, and narrower ripped strips to support them. A 1x2 scrap block is worth cutting specifically to keep the two slide halves parallel while you install them — a slide installed even slightly out of parallel with its partner is the most common reason a "finished" pull-out drawer doesn't glide the way it should.

tools.note: Confirms the cabinet opening is genuinely square before you start — the whole technique depends on it.
Rips the plywood to width for the box parts and support strips.

materials.name: 1/2" plywood, ripped to your opening's width minus 1"
materials.note: Builds the drawer box shelves, front, and back.

materials.name: Full-extension drawer slides, sized to your cabinet's opening depth
1x2 scrap, for a parallel-alignment guide block
Drawer face and knob, sized to fit the opening

steps.title: Check the cabinet before you start
steps.body:
This only works well in a cabinet that meets a few conditions. The opening must be genuinely square — a drawer that slides in and out is unforgiving of a racked opening, so build meticulously square and use straight wood; shimming the slides afterward should be a last resort, not the plan.

Wherever the drawer slides mount (usually the inside faces of the cabinet sides) needs to be flush with the face framing, or the slide can't extend fully without hitting it — shim this if needed. Side-mounted slides work best in an opening that's taller than it is wide, and you'll need to judge how many slide pairs the drawer needs to be well supported. Finally, make sure you'll have room to actually install the slides — if the area will be closed in once you're done, consider installing the slides while it's still accessible.

steps.title: Build the pull-out drawer box
steps.body:
Measure the depth of the cabinet opening and buy full-extension drawer slides in a length that suits it — account for your drawer face thickness (typically 3/4") when deciding the drawer box's overall length, since the face adds to what has to clear the opening.

Measure the height and width of the opening and build the drawer box 1" smaller than the opening in both dimensions — this gives the slides clearance. Rip 1/2" plywood to that width for the shelves, front, and back, and support the shelves with more 1/2" plywood ripped into 2-1/2"-wide strips. Solid dimensional lumber works too; the box doesn't have to be exactly 1" smaller, but don't go tighter than that or you'll fight the slides.

With the box built, separate the two halves of each slide. Screw the drawer-side member to the drawer box, using a 1x2 scrap block to keep both slides parallel as you install them — this matters more than almost any other step. Do the top slide first, then the bottom, taking the same care with parallel alignment both times.
materials: 1/2" plywood, ripped to your opening's width minus 1"
Full-extension drawer slides, sized to your cabinet's opening depth
1x2 scrap, for a parallel-alignment guide block

steps.title: Install the slides in the cabinet
steps.body:
Plan for a 1/2" gap around all sides of the drawer, including the bottom — a scrap piece of 1/2" plywood on the floor of the opening gives you that bottom gap to rest the drawer on (this only works if your face framing is genuinely level; check with a level first).

Rest the drawer, with its slide halves already attached, on the spacer, positioned inset 3/4" to leave room for the drawer face you'll add later. Carefully mark the slide locations on the inside of the cabinet, then pull the drawer back out, leaving the cabinet-side slide members in place. Match those members up with your marks and screw them to the cabinet interior. Most slides have some built-in adjustment if they don't glide perfectly on the first try.

steps.title: Attach the drawer face
steps.body: Hold the drawer face in place with an even gap on all sides and screw it on from behind. If your knobs are sized for a standard 3/4" drawer face and the pull-out box adds extra thickness behind it, you may need to remove one mounting screw, install the knob, then reinstall the face in the exact same position to keep everything aligned.
materials: Drawer face and knob, sized to fit the opening

images.alt: Pull-out drawer retrofit installed in an existing cabinet opening
